## INTAKE BRIEF

```
INTAKE BRIEF
============
Topic: Methods, theory, and failure cases of reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF)
and preference learning as techniques for aligning the behavior of large language models.

Anchor vocabulary: "RLHF" (ML/alignment tradition), "preference learning" (ML and RL
tradition), "aligning language models" (AI safety / alignment tradition)

Candidate vocabulary traditions:
  1. ML/Alignment pipeline — RLHF, reward modeling, PPO, DPO, KTO, SFT, preference
     optimization, InstructGPT, proximal policy optimization
  2. AI Safety / Alignment theory — scalable oversight, constitutional AI, RLAIF, debate,
     amplification, iterated distillation, corrigibility, value alignment
  3. Reinforcement learning theory — reward hacking, Goodhart's law, reward misspecification,
     inverse RL, reward overoptimization, KL divergence penalty
  4. NLP / LLM systems — instruction tuning, supervised fine-tuning, FLAN, InstructGPT,
     chat models, behavioral evaluation, helpfulness, harmlessness
  5. Machine learning theory — Bradley-Terry model, Plackett-Luce, PAC learning, preference
     elicitation, sample complexity, regret bounds, comparative judgments
  6. Decision theory / economics — social choice, Arrow's impossibility theorem, preference
     aggregation, utility functions, revealed preference, value pluralism
  7. HCI / crowdsourcing — annotator agreement, labeler behavior, crowdsourcing quality,
     inter-annotator agreement, annotation pipelines, label noise

Absent-term signal (terms strongly associated with this topic that the user did NOT use):
  - "DPO" / Direct Preference Optimization (major algorithmic alternative to RLHF)
  - "reward hacking" (core failure mode of RLHF)
  - "Goodhart's law" (theoretical grounding for reward misspecification)
  - "Bradley-Terry model" (statistical foundation for pairwise preference learning)
  - "scalable oversight" (AI safety framing of the supervision problem)
  - "constitutional AI" / "RLAIF" (major industry variant of RLHF)

NOTE: Vocabulary traditions listed here are LLM self-reported. The LLM cannot enumerate
traditions it has not encountered. Vocabulary-discovery pass (pearl-growing from a known
anchor paper) is recommended before accepting the angle list as covering all traditions.

Question type: EXPLORATORY
  (No explicit comparison or intervention-outcome structure in the prompt. The user states
  "I want to research" — open space mapping, not a comparative claim.)

Scope markers: None stated — inferred scope: methods, theory, empirical findings, and
failure modes of RLHF and preference learning for LLM alignment; no date range restriction
stated; no model-family restriction stated.

Known exclusions: None stated

Domain maturity estimate: MIXED
  Basis: anchor vocabulary is academic ("RLHF," "preference learning") but the domain
  also has major industry technical reports (InstructGPT, Constitutional AI, Gemini), large
  open-source implementations (TRL, trlX, OpenRLHF on GitHub), and active practitioner
  communities. Academic-heavy for theory; practitioner-heavy for implementation.

Survey paper status: Source B UNAVAILABLE — cannot probe for survey papers in this
execution context. Vocabulary-discovery pass recommended before finalizing angle list.

Source B status: UNAVAILABLE — MODERATE (Source A+C produced 7 distinct vocabulary
traditions; sufficient for angle generation with acknowledged coverage risk)
```

No survey-paper probe was run. The traditions listed represent what is well-covered in LLM
training data for this domain. There is no external validation that all relevant traditions

## COVERAGE MAP

```
COVERAGE MAP
============

Tradition 1: RLHF pipeline — reward model training
  Representative terms: reward model, reward modeling, Bradley-Terry, preference pairs,
    pairwise comparison, RM training, reward signal
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A1
  Gap flag: No — primary tradition; anchor coverage.

Tradition 2: Alignment optimization — offline methods (DPO and variants)
  Representative terms: direct preference optimization, DPO, IPO, KTO, SLiC, offline
    alignment, implicit reward, contrastive preference optimization
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A2
  Gap flag: No — rapidly-growing sub-literature from 2023 onward.

Tradition 3: RL policy optimization — LLM fine-tuning
  Representative terms: PPO, proximal policy optimization, TRPO, policy gradient, online
    RL, KL divergence penalty, reference model, REINFORCE
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A3
  Gap flag: No — core engineering literature.

Tradition 4: AI safety — scalable oversight
  Representative terms: scalable oversight, debate, amplification, iterated distillation,
    weak-to-strong generalization, AI safety, IDA
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A4
  Gap flag: No — distinct from RLHF engineering; focuses on verification problem.

Tradition 5: Constitutional AI / RLAIF — AI-generated feedback
  Representative terms: constitutional AI, RLAIF, AI feedback, AI judge, LLM-as-judge,
    self-critique, red-teaming, harmlessness principles, AI-generated labels
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A5
  Gap flag: No — distinct from scalable oversight; focuses on replacing human labelers.

Tradition 6: Alignment theory — reward misspecification and Goodhart's law
  Representative terms: Goodhart's law, reward hacking, reward overoptimization, proxy
    gaming, specification gaming, reward misspecification, sycophancy, KL penalty
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A6
  Gap flag: No — foundational failure-mode literature.

Tradition 7: NLP/LLM systems — instruction tuning and SFT
  Representative terms: instruction tuning, FLAN, InstructGPT, supervised fine-tuning,
    SFT, chat model, behavioral alignment, zero-shot, few-shot, prompt following
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A7
  Gap flag: No — establishes baseline against which RLHF is compared.

Tradition 8: ML theory — statistical preference learning
  Representative terms: Bradley-Terry model, Plackett-Luce, PAC learning, sample
    complexity, regret bounds, maximum likelihood preference learning, choice models
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A8
  Gap flag: No — provides theoretical foundations largely absent in engineering angles.

Tradition 9: Decision theory / economics — social choice
  Representative terms: social choice theory, Arrow's impossibility, preference
    aggregation, value pluralism, collective preferences, democratic AI, Condorcet
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A9
  Gap flag: No — adjacent discipline with direct relevance to multi-user alignment.

Tradition 10: HCI / crowdsourcing — annotation quality
  Representative terms: annotator agreement, inter-rater reliability, crowdsourcing,
    worker quality, label noise, Mechanical Turk, annotation consistency, rater bias
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A10
  Gap flag: No — production-quality RLHF depends critically on this literature.

Tradition 11: AI fairness — demographic bias in RLHF
  Representative terms: algorithmic fairness, disparate impact, demographic bias, reward
    model bias, societal harms, representation, RLHF fairness evaluation
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A11
  Gap flag: No — growing literature on downstream harms of misaligned reward signals.

Tradition 12: NLP evaluation — alignment assessment benchmarks
  Representative terms: MT-Bench, AlpacaEval, TruthfulQA, HarmBench, MMLU, BBH,
    LLM-as-judge, win rate, alignment evaluation, instruction following metrics
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A12
  Gap flag: No — evaluation methodology is a distinct sub-literature.

Tradition 13: Data efficiency — preference data collection
  Representative terms: active learning, annotation budget, synthetic preferences,
    data augmentation, weak supervision, self-play, preference data generation
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A13
  Gap flag: No — critical practical bottleneck for RLHF deployment at scale.

Tradition 14: Reward learning theory — inverse RL
  Representative terms: inverse reinforcement learning, reward identification, maximum
    entropy IRL, Bayesian IRL, imitation learning, reward recovery, demonstration data
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A14
  Gap flag: No — provides alternative theoretical frame from classical RL.

Tradition 15: Mechanistic interpretability — reward and value encoding
  Representative terms: mechanistic interpretability, circuits, probing, representation
    analysis, feature attribution, value encoding, reward model internals, superposition
  Planned angle slot: Confirmatory A15
  Gap flag: No — emerging literature on what reward models actually learn.

Traditions NOT yet represented in the planned confirmatory list:
  - Cognitive science / judgment and decision-making (how humans form preferences under
    uncertainty): addressed by cross-disciplinary slot C1
  - Red-teaming / adversarial robustness: partially covered by A6 and the adversarial slot
  - Philosophy of value / metaethics: not covered — scope acceptance: primarily empirical
    and engineering topic; metaethics literature unlikely to be retrieved by standard
    search terms and is out of scope for this research question

Cross-disciplinary slot (required):
  Target tradition: Behavioral economics and cognitive psychology — preference elicitation
    under uncertainty, cognitive biases, framing effects, preference reversals
  Basis for selection: RLHF assumes human preferences are reliable ground truth, but
    behavioral economics documents systematic deviations (anchoring, framing effects,
    preference reversals, order effects) that would undermine RLHF's foundational
    assumption. This literature does not use "RLHF" vocabulary and will not appear in
    standard alignment searches.
```

## ANGLE LIST

---

ANGLE 1: Reward Model Training, Architectures, and Generalization
==================================================================
Vocabulary tradition: RLHF pipeline — reward model training
Angle type: Confirmatory
Predicted research character: empirical
What to extract: Reward model architectures, training objectives (pairwise vs. listwise),
  generalization to out-of-distribution prompts, calibration and uncertainty, correlation
  with human judgment, failure modes of specific RM designs.

Source type (academic): arXiv cs.CL + cs.LG
Source type (practitioner): GitHub — search repositories implementing reward models
  (TRL, trlX, OpenRLHF); README vocabulary reveals practitioner design decisions

Queries:
  Q1: reward model training RLHF pairwise preference language models generalization 2023 2024 2025
  Q2: reward model architecture Bradley-Terry training human feedback calibration out-of-distribution
  Q3: preference model uncertainty estimation reward learning language model overfit

Coverage-gap note: This angle does not cover reward model ensembling or active probing
  of reward model disagreement as a signal — those would require a separate data-efficiency
  angle (see A13).

---

ANGLE 2: Direct Preference Optimization and Offline Alignment Algorithms
=========================================================================
Vocabulary tradition: Alignment optimization — offline methods (DPO and variants)
Angle type: Confirmatory
Predicted research character: empirical
What to extract: DPO derivation and assumptions, comparison with PPO-based RLHF,
  variants (IPO, KTO, SLiC, SimPO), scope conditions where offline methods succeed or
  fail versus online methods, implicit reward interpretation.

Source type (academic): arXiv cs.CL + cs.LG
Source type (practitioner): GitHub — implementations of DPO fine-tuning pipelines;
  issue threads reveal practitioner failure modes and hyperparameter sensitivities

Queries:
  Q1: direct preference optimization DPO language model alignment offline reward model free 2023 2024
  Q2: DPO IPO KTO SLiC offline preference optimization comparison LLM alignment 2024 2025
  Q3: implicit reward DPO RLHF without reward model alignment language model theoretical analysis

Coverage-gap note: Does not cover the relationship between offline alignment and
  distribution shift over repeated fine-tuning iterations.

---

ANGLE 3: PPO and Online RL Fine-tuning — Training Dynamics and Stability
=========================================================================
Vocabulary tradition: RL policy optimization — LLM fine-tuning
Angle type: Confirmatory
Predicted research character: empirical
What to extract: Training stability of PPO for LLMs, KL penalty configuration, reference
  model role, reward model overoptimization during online training, engineering decisions
  that make online RLHF work at scale, failure modes documented in large-scale training.

Source type (academic): arXiv cs.LG + cs.CL
Source type (practitioner): GitHub — OpenRLHF, TRL PPO implementations; technical
  reports from OpenAI (InstructGPT), Anthropic, and DeepMind

Queries:
  Q1: proximal policy optimization PPO RLHF language model fine-tuning training stability KL penalty
  Q2: online reinforcement learning large language model policy gradient reward overoptimization
  Q3: RLHF training instability reward hacking PPO engineering alignment LLM empirical 2023 2024

Coverage-gap note: Does not cover actor-critic variants beyond PPO (e.g., GRPO, ReMax,
  REINFORCE++ which emerged in 2024–2025) — those are adjacent and may require a
  separate angle for recent literature.

==================================================================================
Vocabulary tradition: Constitutional AI / RLAIF — AI-generated feedback
Angle type: Confirmatory

ANGLE 6: Reward Misspecification, Reward Overoptimization, and Goodhart's Law
===============================================================================
Vocabulary tradition: Alignment theory — reward misspecification and Goodhart's law
Angle type: Confirmatory
Predicted research character: empirical
What to extract: Theoretical formulations of Goodhart's law in ML, empirical measurements
  of reward overoptimization as a function of KL divergence, scope conditions for reward
  hacking in language models, mitigation strategies, proxy gaming behaviors observed.

Source type (academic): arXiv cs.LG + cs.CL + cs.AI
Source type (practitioner): Alignment Forum posts and LessWrong (the origin community
  for Goodhart's law in AI); GitHub issue threads in RLHF repositories documenting
  observed hacking behaviors

Queries:
  Q1: reward overoptimization Goodhart law RLHF language model empirical KL divergence 2022 2023 2024
  Q2: reward misspecification proxy gaming specification gaming AI alignment language model
  Q3: sycophancy reward hacking aligned language model overoptimization failure empirical evaluation

Coverage-gap note: Does not cover the theoretical conditions under which Goodhart's law
  can be avoided — that requires a more formal learning-theory treatment (Angle 8).

ANGLE 7: Instruction Tuning and Supervised Fine-tuning as Alignment Baselines
===============================================================================
Vocabulary tradition: NLP/LLM systems — instruction tuning and supervised fine-tuning
Angle type: Confirmatory
Predicted research character: empirical
What to extract: SFT as the pre-RLHF step and as a standalone alignment method, FLAN
  and instruction-following corpora, comparison of SFT-only versus SFT+RLHF, behavioral
  differences, failure modes of pure SFT for alignment.

Source type (academic): arXiv cs.CL + cs.LG
Source type (practitioner): GitHub — instruction-tuning datasets (Alpaca, Dolly,
  OpenAssistant); HuggingFace model cards documenting SFT-only aligned models

Queries:
  Q1: instruction tuning language models FLAN InstructGPT supervised fine-tuning alignment baseline
  Q2: SFT versus RLHF comparison alignment instruction following language model behavioral difference
  Q3: supervised fine-tuning alignment limitation failure mode LLM helpfulness honesty harmlessness

Coverage-gap note: Does not cover distillation-based alignment (LIMA, Alpagasus) which
  emerged as a low-data SFT variant — that would require a focused angle.

ANGLE 8: Statistical and Theoretical Analysis of Preference Learning
=====================================================================
Vocabulary tradition: ML theory — statistical preference learning
Angle type: Confirmatory
Predicted research character: empirical
What to extract: Theoretical guarantees for preference learning, sample complexity of
  learning a reward function from pairwise comparisons, identifiability of reward under
  Bradley-Terry and Plackett-Luce models, regret bounds, conditions for PAC-learnability.

Source type (academic): arXiv cs.LG + stat.ML (theoretical ML community; NOT cs.CL)
Source type (practitioner): deprioritized — this is a theory-heavy angle with minimal
  practitioner production; Semantic Scholar for citation-network exploration of foundational
  papers

Queries:
  Q1: preference learning sample complexity theoretical analysis Bradley-Terry model reward 2022 2023 2024
  Q2: Plackett-Luce choice model reward learning identifiability theoretical foundations language model
  Q3: PAC learning pairwise preferences regret bounds reward function estimation statistical

Coverage-gap note: Does not cover online preference learning (active elicitation) theory —
  see Angle 13 for the data-efficiency / active-learning treatment.

==================================================================================
Vocabulary tradition: Decision theory / economics — social choice and value aggregation
Angle type: Confirmatory
Predicted research character: empirical
What to extract: Social choice impossibility theorems applied to alignment, methods for
  aggregating diverse human preferences, implications of value plurality for RLHF,
  democratic or participatory approaches to AI alignment, empirical work on preference
  heterogeneity in annotation.

Source type (academic): arXiv cs.AI + cs.GT (game theory); Journal of Artificial
  Intelligence Research (JAIR); also check economics journals (Journal of Economic Theory)
Source type (practitioner): Alignment Forum / AI safety community discussions; DeepMind
  and Anthropic technical reports on collective alignment

  Q2: collective preferences AI alignment Arrow impossibility theorem value diversity aggregation LLM
  Q3: participatory AI alignment stakeholder preferences heterogeneous values RLHF human feedback

ANGLE 14: Inverse Reinforcement Learning and Reward Identification from Demonstrations
========================================================================================
Vocabulary tradition: Reward learning theory — inverse RL and Bayesian reward inference
Angle type: Confirmatory
Predicted research character: empirical
What to extract: IRL theoretical foundations (maximum entropy IRL, Bayesian IRL),
  applicability to LLM alignment, reward identification vs. reward recovery, connection
  between IRL and RLHF preference learning, limitations of demonstration-based reward
  learning.

Source type (academic): arXiv cs.LG + cs.RO (robotics, where IRL originated); ICML
  and NeurIPS proceedings
Source type (practitioner): deprioritized — primarily theoretical / robotics literature
  with limited LLM-specific practitioner production; Semantic Scholar for forward
  citation search from Ziebart and Ng foundational papers

Queries:
  Q1: inverse reinforcement learning reward identification language model alignment demonstration 2020 2023 2024
  Q2: Bayesian inverse RL reward learning from preferences maximum entropy alignment foundation
  Q3: reward learning demonstrations imitation learning IRL connection RLHF language model theoretical

Coverage-gap note: Does not cover causal identification of reward functions — whether
  RLHF preference data is sufficient to identify the "true" reward versus multiple
  reward functions consistent with the data.

ANGLE 16 (N1): Evidence that RLHF and Preference Learning Fail, Do Not Generalize, or Cause Harm
==================================================================================================
Vocabulary tradition: RLHF pipeline — disconfirming variant (null-hypothesis angle)
Angle type: Null-hypothesis
Predicted research character: empirical
What to extract: Documented failure modes of RLHF in deployment, negative results on
  generalization of reward models across domains, unintended behavioral consequences
  (sycophancy, verbosity bias, refusal over-generalization), cases where RLHF reduced
  truthfulness or capability, papers documenting that RLHF does not produce the claimed
  alignment properties.

Source type (academic): arXiv cs.CL + cs.LG — search for failure terms explicitly
Source type (practitioner): GitHub issues in major RLHF frameworks documenting observed
  failures; AI safety community Alignment Forum posts on RLHF limitations

Queries:
  Q1: RLHF failure limitations alignment does not generalize negative results language model 2022 2023 2024
  Q2: reinforcement learning human feedback unintended consequences harm sycophancy verbosity bias aligned LLM
  Q3: preference learning RLHF limitations sycophancy misalignment reward hacking empirical critique evaluation

Coverage-gap note: This angle specifically seeks disconfirming evidence. Papers that
  partially succeed but document scope conditions for failure are equally relevant to
  papers documenting complete failure.

ANGLE 17 (D1) [ADVERSARIAL-FLAGGED: dispatch in separate research context with no prior confirmatory results]
==============================================================================================================
Title: Alignment Tax, Capability Suppression, and Structural Critiques of RLHF as an Alignment Paradigm
=========================================================================================================
Vocabulary tradition: Critical AI studies — alignment critique and capability-alignment tradeoffs
Angle type: Adversarial-FLAGGED
Predicted research character: empirical
What to extract: Evidence that safety fine-tuning reduces capability, that RLHF produces
  superficial compliance rather than value internalization, structural critiques of RLHF
  as an alignment paradigm (versus alternatives), industry criticism of RLHF claims,
  arguments that RLHF optimizes for user approval proxies rather than actual alignment.

Starting vocabulary (NOT derived from confirmatory angle vocabulary):
  Excluded from generation: RLHF, reward model, preference optimization, DPO, PPO,
  Bradley-Terry, scalable oversight, constitutional AI, RLAIF, instruction tuning
  Starting terms used: alignment tax, capability regression, safety fine-tuning tradeoff,
  superficial compliance, sycophancy critique, approval-seeking, Potemkin alignment,
  alignment washing, goodharting

Source type (academic): arXiv cs.CL + cs.AI — search for critique and failure terms;
  NeurIPS workshop papers (more likely to publish contrarian findings than main venues)
Source type (practitioner): Alignment Forum (critical AI safety community); Twitter/X
  threads from ML researchers critiquing RLHF results; ACM FAccT critical AI papers

Queries:
  Q1: alignment tax capability degradation safety fine-tuning tradeoff language model performance 2023 2024
  Q2: sycophancy approval-seeking aligned LLM user satisfaction proxy Goodhart superficial compliance
  Q3: RLHF critique structural limitations alignment paradigm alternative approaches failure critique 2023 2024

Coverage-gap note: This angle should be dispatched WITHOUT prior exposure to the 15
  confirmatory angle results. The adversarial framing is only preserved if the research
  context has not already been primed by confirmatory RLHF literature.

---

ANGLE 18 (C1): Behavioral Economics and Cognitive Psychology of Human Preference Elicitation
=============================================================================================
Vocabulary tradition: Behavioral economics / cognitive psychology — preference expression
  under uncertainty (cross-disciplinary — NOT in intake brief's candidate traditions)
Angle type: Cross-disciplinary
Predicted research character: empirical
What to extract: Cognitive biases that affect how humans express preferences in pairwise
  comparison tasks (anchoring, framing, order effects, recency bias), preference
  reversals and inconsistency, implications for RLHF reliability, whether preference
  elicitation tasks used in RLHF produce stable ground-truth signal, behavioral economics
  critiques of revealed preference assumptions.

Source type (academic): Journal of Behavioral Decision Making; Psychological Review;
  Judgment and Decision Making journal; PubMed (search psychological terms); Semantic
  Scholar (to bridge behavioral econ → AI)
Source type (practitioner): HCI conference proceedings (CHI) on annotation task design;
  Scale AI and Surge AI documentation on annotator training (if publicly available)

Queries:
  Q1: preference elicitation cognitive bias anchoring framing order effects pairwise comparison human judgment
  Q2: preference reversal inconsistency behavioral economics human feedback reliability annotation AI training
  Q3: revealed preference stated preference inconsistency behavioral annotation quality crowdsourcing bias cognitive

Coverage-gap note: This angle searches primarily non-AI databases. The cross-disciplinary
  connection may require manual bridging: find behavioral economics results on preference
  consistency, then reason about their implications for RLHF — direct citations may be
  rare.

---

## GATE SUMMARY

```
GATE SUMMARY
============

Gate 1 — Completeness:
  [x] Null-hypothesis angle present: PASS — Angle 16 (N1) explicitly frames the
      disconfirming search; queries use "failure," "limitations," "does not generalize,"
      "negative results," "sycophancy," "misalignment"
  [x] Adversarial angle present: PASS — Angle 17 (D1) labeled ADVERSARIAL-FLAGGED with
      dispatch note; starting vocabulary explicitly separated from confirmatory angles
  [x] Cross-disciplinary angle present: PASS — Angle 18 (C1) targets behavioral
      economics / cognitive psychology, which is NOT in the intake brief's candidate
      traditions list
  [x] Source-type coverage: PASS — Domain flagged as MIXED; multiple angles assign
      practitioner sources (GitHub, technical reports, Alignment Forum): Angles 1, 2, 3,
      5, 6, 10, 12, 13, 15, 17, 18
  [x] Vocabulary-tradition map populated: PASS — Coverage map has 15 distinct tradition
      entries from intake brief; all have planned angle slots. 2 additional traditions
      in adversarial and cross-disciplinary slots.

Gate 1 verdict: PASS

Gate 2 — Distinctness:
  Boolean-connective test (representative pair checks):
  - A1 (reward model training) vs. A2 (DPO offline methods): A1 queries use "reward model
    training," "Bradley-Terry," "overfit"; A2 queries use "offline," "DPO," "implicit
    reward." OR would not retrieve the same documents. PASS.
  - A4 (scalable oversight) vs. A5 (constitutional AI/RLAIF): A4 queries use "debate,"
    "amplification," "weak-to-strong generalization"; A5 queries use "constitutional AI,"
    "self-critique," "AI-generated preference." Different paper populations. PASS.
  - A6 (reward misspecification) vs. N1 (null-hypothesis): A6 confirmatory on Goodhart's
    law; N1 explicitly disconfirming across all failure types. N1 is the null-hypothesis
    variant of A6 and other angles — exempted from merge requirement per Gate 2 rules.
  - A7 (instruction tuning) vs. A12 (evaluation benchmarks): A7 queries use "FLAN,"
    "SFT," "supervised fine-tuning," "training"; A12 queries use "MT-Bench,"
    "AlpacaEval," "LLM-as-judge." Different paper populations. PASS.
  - A3 (PPO online RL) vs. A14 (inverse RL): A3 queries use "PPO," "policy gradient,"
    "online RL," "KL penalty"; A14 queries use "inverse reinforcement learning,"
    "Bayesian IRL," "demonstration." Distinct traditions. PASS.
  Vocabulary-tradition distinctness: All 18 angles have distinct tradition labels. No
    two non-null-hypothesis, non-adversarial angles share an identical tradition label.
    PASS.

Gate 2 verdict: PASS

Gate 3 — Launchability:
  Spot-checked all 18 angles:
  - All angles contain ≥3 concrete query strings that can be pasted into a search box
  - No query string contains "TBD," "to be determined," "[fill in]," or placeholder text
  - All angles name specific academic sources (arXiv with category, JAIR, CHI, etc.)
    and specific practitioner sources (GitHub, Alignment Forum, HuggingFace)
  PASS across all 18 angles.

Gate 3 verdict: PASS

Gate 4 — Scoring prohibition:
  No angles were ranked, filtered, or prioritized during generation. No phrases such as
  "this angle is most useful" or "this is the strongest angle" appear in any angle record.
  Structural criteria only were used: vocabulary-tradition coverage, source-type coverage,
  null-hypothesis presence, Boolean-connective distinctness.

Gate 4 verdict: PASS

Overall verdict: PASS — 18 angles are dispatch-ready.

Flags requiring human judgment:
  1. VOCABULARY SELF-REPORT FLAG: All 15 traditions at the intake stage and the
     coverage-map expansion to 17 traditions were enumerated by LLM only. No survey paper
     probe was run (Source B unavailable). Traditions that are underrepresented in LLM
     training data for this domain may have been missed. Recommend running a survey-paper
     search ("RLHF survey 2024 2025," "preference learning alignment survey 2024") to
     identify additional vocabulary terms before treating this list as coverage-complete.
  2. Adversarial execution discipline: Angle 17 (D1) is labeled ADVERSARIAL-FLAGGED.
     The downstream research agent must honor this by executing Angle 17 in a separate
     context with no prior exposure to the 15 confirmatory angle results. This kit
     cannot enforce that at execution time.
  3. Cross-disciplinary bridge (Angle 18): Behavioral economics literature does not
     cite RLHF by name. Manual bridging from preference-elicitation psychology results
     to RLHF implications is required — automated extraction will be incomplete here.
  4. Adjacent tradition not covered: Philosophy of value / metaethics. This was assessed
     as out of scope (scope-acceptance statement: not covered because it requires a
     different research question framing and the empirical literature is unlikely to be
     retrieved by standard database search). If the user's question extends to normative
     foundations of value alignment, this requires an additional angle.
  5. Temporal currency: This is an active research area (2023–2025 saw major shifts:
     DPO publication, RLAIF scaling, weak-to-strong generalization). Angles 3, 4, 5,
     and 12 are particularly likely to have significant new literature in 2025. Apply
     date filters explicitly.
```
